items.title: The two loops of Hermes Agent
items.description:
Why the closed learning loop needs an open one — and what that tells us about the limits of agentic self-improvement.

Hermes Agent ships two repositories. One you install. The other you don’t.

The main one — over 130,000 GitHub stars in months, MIT-licensed, Nous Research’s flagship open-source agent — implements what its README calls a built-in learning loop. Skills get created from experience after sufficiently complex tasks. They get patched during use when they prove outdated or wrong. A background Curator grades, consolidates, and archives them on a recurring cycle. Past sessions are searchable through full-text indexing. A persistent user model deepens across sessions through Honcho’s dialectic modeling. The closed loop is the product, and the product is good enough that the project crossed six figures in stars faster than almost anything else in open-source AI.

The second repository is called hermes-agent-self-evolution. It runs offline. It reads execution traces. It runs an evolutionary prompt search called GEPA — Genetic-Pareto Prompt Evolution — and it lives outside the runtime on purpose. It exists because the closed loop has a defect that the community has confirmed: the agent thinks it did well almost every time, even when it didn't. The same mechanism that produces skills can also overwrite manual customizations with worse versions of themselves.

That second repository is not an embarrassment. It is the most important architectural decision Nous made, and most readers of the README are going to miss it. Hermes Agent is the strongest open-source implementation of harness engineering we have. The existence of hermes-agent-self-evolution proves that harness without external judgment is not a closed loop. It is an open loop with a different name.

This essay is about why that matters — for how we read Hermes, and for how we build the next thing.

The harness, materialized

I’ve written before about agentic systems as harness engineering: the work of designing the scaffolding around the model rather than the model itself. The wider harness has six dimensions — instructions, constraints, feedback, memory, orchestration, and the human judgment layer that sits across all of them. Most production agents I see treat these as runtime concerns, hidden inside prompt templates and middleware. They are real, but they are not legible. You cannot version them. You cannot diff them. You cannot hand them to a colleague.

Hermes Agent’s first achievement is that it makes the harness legible. The dimensions become files.

Instructions and identity live in a soul document and the system prompt builder. Memory splits into two layers: memory.md for facts, preferences, and lessons learned, and user.md for the working model of who you are. Skills are procedural memory — Markdown documents with YAML front matter, stored in ~/.hermes/skills/, with explicit triggers and execution code. Orchestration spans cron schedules, subagent spawns, and an 18-platform messaging gateway. Feedback comes through tool results, FTS5 retrieval over past sessions, and the Curator's grading pass.

Each of these is a file on disk. You can read them. You can edit them. You can put them in git. The agent’s “memory” is not a vector embedding you have to guess at — it is a directory tree you can ls. The skills the agent creates after solving a problem are not opaque updates to weights — they are documents you can open in Neovim and rewrite.

This sounds like an obvious design choice, but it isn’t. The dominant pattern in commercial agentic platforms is to bury the harness inside the runtime, expose only the chat surface, and treat the underlying state as proprietary. Hermes inverts that. The harness is the product. The model is the substrate.

For someone like me who has spent the last year arguing that the interesting work in agentic engineering is the work around the model, Hermes is the cleanest existence proof I’ve seen that the argument is right. The agent gets better over time not because the weights change but because the files change. Skills accumulate. Memory deepens. The user model sharpens. None of that requires fine-tuning. All of it requires writing.

What the closed loop actually does

The mechanism is worth describing precisely, because the precision is where the cracks show.

After a task that involves five or more tool calls, the agent evaluates whether the trajectory it just executed should be captured as a reusable skill. If yes, it writes a Markdown document with the conditions under which the skill should be triggered, the steps to execute, and any constraints it noticed during execution. The skill goes into the user’s local skill library.

During subsequent sessions, when the agent recognizes a similar problem, it retrieves the skill and follows it. If, during execution, the skill turns out to be incomplete, outdated, or wrong, the agent patches the document in place. This is the self-improvement claim, and it is real. Skills do get better with use, the way runbooks at a well-run operations team get better with each post-mortem.

The Curator is the third piece, and it’s the most interesting one. The Curator is a background process that periodically reviews the skill library, tracks how often each skill is viewed, used, and patched, moves long-unused skills through active → stale → archived states, and spawns a short auxiliary-model review that proposes consolidations when it spots near-duplicates. It never auto-deletes — the worst outcome is archival into a recoverable directory. It never touches bundled or hub-installed skills, only the ones the agent itself authored. Users can pin critical skills to fence them off from archival.

This is governance of procedural memory, and it is something I have not seen any other open-source agent treat as a first-class concern. Without the Curator, the skill library accumulates dozens of narrow near-duplicates over time and starts to pollute the context window with retrieval noise. The Curator is the librarian that keeps the library navigable. It is the difference between a knowledge base and a junk drawer.

So far, this is a story about a well-designed system. Skills as first-class procedural memory. Curator as memory governance. Files as the interface. Six harness dimensions, materialized.

And then we have to talk about who grades the work.

The self-congratulation problem

There is a structural defect in the closed loop, and the Hermes community has documented it openly. The agent grades its own performance. When deciding whether a task deserves a skill, when deciding whether a skill needs patching, when deciding whether two skills should be consolidated, the evaluator is the same kind of model that did the work.

The defect is what you would predict. The agent thinks it did well almost every time. Skills get created from trajectories that didn’t actually succeed. Patches overwrite correct logic with logic the model retroactively prefers. Manual customizations the user wrote get replaced with worse autogenerated versions, because the autogenerated version flatters itself more confidently than the human’s prose argues for itself.

This is grading-by-the-grader, and it is not a bug specific to Hermes. It is a structural feature of any system where an LLM is asked to evaluate the output of an LLM. The evaluator and the executor share the same priors. They share the same failure modes. They share the same blind spots about what they don’t know. Asking the agent “did you do well?” is asking the agent to perform a coherent narrative about its own competence, and LLMs are very good at coherent narrative about their own competence.

The Hermes team knows this. The community knows this. And the answer they shipped is the second repository.

GEPA as architectural confession

hermes-agent-self-evolution lives outside the runtime. It reads the agent's execution traces — the actual record of what got called, what got returned, what got retried — and it runs an evolutionary search over prompt variants using a Genetic-Pareto algorithm. The objective is not "did the agent think it did well" but "what does the trace itself say about where things failed." It proposes targeted improvements. Humans review.

The choice to keep this outside the main runtime is the part worth dwelling on. It would have been technically simpler to bundle GEPA into the closed loop and call it self-improvement v2. They didn’t. They split it into a separate repository, with a separate cadence, a separate set of inputs (traces, not self-reports), and a separate signature for the proposed changes.

That split is an architectural confession. It says: the in-runtime loop cannot grade itself, because the grader shares the executor’s priors. The grading has to happen elsewhere, on a different temporal cycle, against a different signal. GEPA reads traces because traces are the closest thing to a ground-truth artifact the system produces. The agent’s self-narrative is unreliable. The record of what it actually did is less so.

This is the part that the marketing copy doesn’t say out loud. The closed loop is not closed. It depends on a second loop — slower, offline, trace-based, human-reviewed — to correct the first loop’s bias toward thinking well of itself. The two-repository structure is the visible shape of that dependency.

What Naur and Polanyi predicted

None of this should surprise anyone who has read Peter Naur on theory-building or Michael Polanyi on tacit knowledge, which is to say it should have surprised no one.

Naur’s argument in Programming as Theory Building is that the artifact of programming is not the code but the theory the programmer holds in their head about why the code is the way it is. The code is a residue. Take the theory away — fire the team, lose the institutional memory — and the code becomes inert, even though it still runs. New people can read it, run it, modify it superficially, but they cannot extend it coherently because the theory that justifies its shape is gone.

Hermes’ learning loop accumulates artifacts. It accumulates skills, memory entries, user-model deltas. What it does not accumulate is theory. The agent does not hold an explanation of why the skill is shaped the way it is, or why a particular consolidation makes sense, or why the patch fixes something the previous version got wrong. It holds the artifact, and it has a coherent-sounding story about the artifact at the moment of creation, but the story is generated fresh each time and the priors are the same priors that produced the artifact in the first place.

Polanyi’s argument is adjacent. Tacit knowledge — the part of competence that the competent practitioner cannot fully articulate — does not survive distillation into explicit rules. When you ask an expert to write down what they do, what you get back is a stylized account that the expert can produce because they are expert, but that someone reading the account cannot use to become expert. The account is the visible tip of an iceberg whose mass is below the waterline.

Asking an LLM-driven agent to self-evaluate is asking it to distill its own tacit competence into an explicit grade. The grade is generated, the grade is plausible, the grade flatters the work. And it has to, because the model doesn’t have any other way to render an opinion about itself than to produce coherent text about itself. The text is the opinion. The opinion is the text.

GEPA’s choice to read traces rather than ask the agent is, in this light, exactly the right move. Traces are external. Traces survive the session. Traces resist self-flattery because they are not narrative — they are record. Reading traces is closer to what Naur would call theory-building from artifacts than asking the agent is. Not because the trace contains theory, but because the trace is the artifact about which a theory can be built — by someone, or something, outside the agent’s own narrative loop.

Where this leaves platform builders

If you are building an agentic platform — and many of us are — Hermes Agent’s two-repository structure is the most useful thing to study, more useful than the skill format or the gateway count.

The lesson is that the question is not “how do we close the loop.” The loop does not close. Anyone telling you their agent has a closed learning loop is either marketing or hasn’t shipped to production yet. The interesting question is: where do we put the seam between the inner loop and the external judgment that corrects it?

Hermes puts the seam between repositories, between runtime and offline, between self-report and trace. That is one valid answer. There are others. You can put the seam between agent and human reviewer, with the human inspecting proposed skill changes before they merge. You can put the seam between agent and another agent of a deliberately different architecture, with the second agent reading the first one’s traces. You can put the seam at the org level — agents propose, teams approve.

But the seam exists. The seam is where the harness gains the property that the agent alone cannot give it: the property of being correctable by something that does not share the agent’s priors.

This is also the answer to a question I keep getting asked: if the harness does the heavy lifting, why do you still need humans? The answer is that the harness, by itself, cannot grade itself either. The harness is just structure. It needs an external observer to maintain its calibration. In small teams, that observer is the person on call. In larger systems, it’s a review process, or a pipeline like GEPA, or a deliberately heterogeneous evaluator. Without it, the harness drifts toward whatever the model finds plausible — which is, structurally, whatever flatters the model’s narrative of competence.

Coda: the agent that grows with you

Nous Research markets Hermes Agent with the phrase the agent that grows with you. It is a good phrase. It is also more accurate than the marketing language usually is, because the “with you” is doing more work than it appears to.

Hermes does not grow on its own. It grows in the presence of a user who pins the skills they care about, edits the ones the Curator gets wrong, runs GEPA when the inner loop starts to drift, and reviews what the offline pipeline proposes before merging. The growth is real. The agent is real. The skills accumulate, the memory deepens, the user model sharpens. But the growth is the joint product of two loops — one inside the agent, one outside — and the outside loop is the one that keeps the inside loop honest.

That is not a weakness of Hermes Agent. It is the design. The two repositories tell you so. The team’s honesty about the self-congratulation bias tells you so. The decision to ship GEPA as a separate companion rather than bury it inside the main runtime tells you so.

Most agentic platforms are going to have to learn this lesson the hard way, by shipping closed loops and watching them drift. Hermes’ contribution is to have learned it early, and to have left the evidence in plain sight for anyone willing to read the second repository as carefully as the first.

The closed loop is not closed. The agent that grows with you grows because you are still in the loop. That’s the architecture. That’s the lesson. The rest is implementation detail.

Daniel Braz is CTO at BRQ Product & Experience Studios. He writes about software engineering, AI agents, and the evolving relationship between human judgment and machine capability.

The two loops of Hermes Agent was originally published in Level Up Coding on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: When everyone can produce, who gets to judge?
items.description:
The standard question about AI and careers is whether the future belongs to generalists or specialists. The reasoning is familiar: a designer can now code, a developer can mock up a screen, a salesperson can draft a business case. The friction between disciplines is collapsing. So pick a side — broad or deep — and bet accordingly.

The question has a problem. It assumes a single continuum, and the answers it produces are tired. The more interesting question is different. AI is not flattening the generalist/specialist trade-off. It is relocating specialization. And that move has a consequence almost nobody is talking about.

What AI actually does

Aristotle distinguished techne — technical skill, the capacity to produce — from phronesis, practical judgment. The distinction matters here.

AI democratizes techne. It does not touch phronesis.

The designer who codes with an LLM produces code-shaped artifacts and mistakes them for code. The surface confirms her: it compiles, it runs, something appears on screen. She has no apparatus to judge whether what compiled will hold up under any kind of pressure — only the model’s claim that it is done. The developer who mocks up a screen produces functional pixels, not composition that sustains attention. The non-engineer who drafts an architecture document produces a credible-looking structure, but cannot see the failure mode a senior engineer would catch in thirty seconds.

This is cargo cult at the individual level. For two decades, executives flew to Silicon Valley to learn how to become tech-native and brought home the visible surface — open offices, beanbags, slides between floors — expecting the capability to follow the artifact. It never did, because the capability was never in the artifact. The same pattern now operates at the level of the individual worker. AI hands you code-shaped, design-shaped, strategy-shaped artifacts. The form is convincing. The judgment that would tell you whether the form means anything is precisely the part the model cannot supply.

So AI expands the range of what each person can produce. It does not raise the ceiling of what each person can judge. That ceiling is still glued to lived experience in the domain — the kind of knowledge Michael Polanyi called tacit: we know more than we can tell. The LLM does not have it, because tacit knowledge is precisely what the corpus systematically excludes. It lives in the hands and the gut, not in the text.

The relocation of value

When everyone can produce, the value of producing collapses. Value migrates one step over, to whoever can decide whether the production is any good.

This is Clayton Christensen’s Law of Conservation of Attractive Profits applied to skills instead of stack layers. Profitability does not vanish when something gets commoditized. It moves to the adjacent layer that is now the bottleneck. The bottleneck of AI-assisted work is not output. It is evaluation.

That is the inversion. The specialist of the future is not the person who knows how to do manually what AI does automatically. That person is becoming an artisan — culturally interesting, economically marginal. The specialist is whoever has accumulated enough depth in a specific domain to evaluate what the model produces there. To spot the subtle error. To see the hidden risk. To recognize the opportunity the model missed because it has never lived anything.

Knowing how to write a SQL query by hand is not specialization. Knowing why this particular query, applied to this particular dataset, will give a misleading answer that a junior would happily ship to production — that is specialization.

The paradox

Specialization, in the sense I just defined, is built by doing the grunt work that AI is now absorbing.

The doctor becomes a specialist by seeing thousands of cases, most of them unremarkable. The senior engineer earns judgment by debugging for years, including the boring debugging. The designer knows when something works because she designed a thousand mediocre interfaces first.

Peter Naur’s framing makes this literal. Programming, in his account, is not the production of code. It is the construction of a theory in the mind of the programmer — a theory of the problem, the domain, the relationships, the edge cases. The code is a residue of that theory, useful only insofar as someone in the room still holds it. When the theory-holder leaves, the code rots, even if nothing in the code has changed.

The theory is built by doing the work. All of it, including the boring parts. There is no shortcut. The tacit knowledge that lets a senior engineer say “this will not survive contact with production” is not transferable through documentation, mentoring, or simulation. It comes from having been wrong enough times in low-stakes situations to recognize the pattern in a high-stakes one.

Now consider what happens if the next generation starts directly at the level of orchestrating AI — skipping the repetitive work that builds the theory. Where do the specialists come from in ten years? Who corrects the model when its mistakes are not obvious?

The paradox: AI may be simultaneously raising the value of real expertise and dismantling the path that produces it. This is not a management problem. It is a structural tension, and I do not think anyone has a clean answer to it yet.

The view from practice

In the studio I run, and in most engineering organizations I talk to, the operational reality is unambiguous. Everyone can “do” everything now. Designers ship code. PMs ship designs. Engineers ship strategy decks. The friction between roles is real, and it is collapsing.

But when something serious happens — a client escalation, a production incident, an architecture decision that will cost millions if it is wrong — the question is never “who can produce a response?” The question is “who actually understands what is happening?” And the person we call is not the generalist who does six things at sixty percent. It is the specialist who sees what the room cannot see.

There are fewer of these specialists than there used to be. There is more demand. The market is telling us where value is going, and it is not toward the average AI-assisted generalist. It is toward the rare individual whose judgment is trusted in a specific domain — and that trust accrues only through years of practice that the current generation of tooling makes optional.

Two strategies, neither easy

If the diagnosis is right, two strategies follow, and neither is the LinkedIn cliché.

The first: if you are early in your career, resist the temptation to skip the grunt work, even when AI makes it trivial to skip. The grunt work is the formation, not a stage you pass through on the way to the work. The theory Naur describes is not built by orchestration. It is built in the debugging, the revision, the hours where you sit with something broken and slowly build a model of why. If you outsource that to a model, you also outsource the formation of your own judgment. You will be productive earlier and irrelevant sooner.

The second: if you lead engineers, designers, product people — anyone whose value depends on judgment — you have a harder problem. You need to give your people room to develop tacit knowledge in a tooling environment that systematically discourages it. That means deliberately preserving moments of friction. Code reviews that are not just AI-approved. Design critiques where the model’s first suggestion is not the starting point. Architecture conversations where the easy answer is not allowed to win. Otherwise you end up running an organization full of people who can produce but cannot evaluate, supervised by a shrinking number of seniors who do not have replacements.

Neither of these strategies is easy to defend in a quarterly review. Both make the obvious metrics worse in the short term. That is part of why they are uncomfortable, and part of why they will be rare.

The reframe

So generalists or specialists.

Generalists with AI will be the median. They will be productive, broad, fast, and largely interchangeable. They will absorb a lot of the work that used to require multiple people. That is not a small thing.

Specialists with AI will be the exception that decides. They will be rare, expensive, and structurally necessary, because everything the median produces still has to be judged by someone — and judgment does not come from prompts.

The trap is in the middle. The trap is the assumption that “doing a bit of everything” is a stable career position, when it is in fact the position AI absorbs first. The way out of the trap is not breadth. It is depth in something, anything, that you choose to take seriously enough to suffer through the formation.

The future does not belong to generalists or specialists. It belongs to whoever still knows how to judge — and to the institutions wise enough to keep producing them.

Daniel Braz is CTO at BRQ Product & Experience Studios, where he leads a digital products studio focused on AI-augmented software engineering. He is currently researching context degradation and specification-driven development in agentic workflows.

When everyone can produce, who gets to judge? was originally published in Level Up Coding on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: Building on a model that can vanish on a Friday night
items.description:
On Friday, June 12, 2026, at 5:21pm ET, Anthropic received a letter from the U.S. Department of Commerce. Hours later, Fable 5 and Mythos 5 were offline — for everyone. Not a product decision, not an outage, not a contract ending. An export-control directive barred access by any foreign national, inside or outside the U.S., and complying selectively was infeasible, so both models went dark entirely.

The stated trigger was almost absurdly narrow: the “jailbreak” amounted to asking the model to read a codebase and fix software flaws — a capability available in other public models. For anyone building product, the question isn’t whether the government was right. It’s the shape of the event: minimal trigger, total shutdown, zero notice.

So the only question that matters if you have something running in production:

How do I design a system knowing my most critical input can be revoked, without warning, for a reason that has nothing to do with me?

The frontier model stopped being a technical dependency and became a geopolitical one. And geopolitical dependencies fail differently than anything we’re used to handling.

A new failure mode: sovereign revocation

We know how to handle most ways an external provider fails, and each has a known mitigation: latency and instability get timeouts, retries, circuit breakers; rate limits get queues and backpressure; announced deprecation gets a migration window; price changes get renegotiation; regional outages get failover.

All of those share one property: they’re either reversible in minutes or they come with warning. Sovereign revocation has neither. It’s instantaneous (effective the moment the letter lands), binary (the model doesn’t degrade, it disappears), exogenous (the cause isn’t your usage, contract, or the provider’s health — you can be the perfect customer and lose access anyway), and indiscriminate (the shutdown hit everyone, not just the order’s nominal target; collateral damage is the mechanism, not the exception).

That combination breaks the playbooks. A circuit breaker is useless for a model that isn’t coming back in 30 seconds. A migration window is useless for an event with no warning. Sovereign revocation looks less like an outage and more like the sudden bankruptcy of a sole supplier — and the right mitigation comes from business continuity, not SRE.

The design principle: the model is interchangeable, not your product’s soul

The most important architectural decision is also the most philosophical, and it precedes any code: your product can’t be about the model. It has to be about what you build around it.

If your differentiator is “we use the world’s most advanced model,” you don’t have a product — you have a wrapper exposed to a foreign government’s regulatory risk. If your differentiator is your proprietary context, your data, your domain, your orchestration, your guardrails, your distribution — then the model is a replaceable part, and replacing it is an engineering operation, not an existential crisis.

The model is the engine, and engines get commoditized. What’s yours and defensible lives in layers no Commerce Department letter can reach: the context and data you inject, how you decompose and chain and validate, your evaluation criteria and policies, and your relationship with the people who use it. Couple those layers to one specific model and you’ve turned defensible assets into hostages of a decision that isn’t yours. Isolate them behind a clean boundary and the engine can swap underneath them.

The concrete architecture: five layers of defense

The principle becomes engineering in five moves, from the obvious to the most neglected.

1. A model abstraction layer (non-negotiable). No part of your product code should know the model’s name. Every call goes through an internal interface — a gateway or your own inference layer — that takes an intent (“classify,” “extract,” “reason about this”) and decides which model serves it. Provider name, model string, model-specific parameters: all of it lives on one side of a boundary. This is the difference between swapping a model being a config flip and being a six-week refactor at the worst possible moment. Anyone already running multi-LLM behind orchestration has done 80% of this without noticing; anyone who wired the provider SDK straight into business logic did the opposite.

2. Heterogeneous fallback, not just redundancy. Classic redundancy — two instances of the same model in two regions — does not protect against sovereign revocation, because the order hits the model, not the region. Failover has to be heterogeneous: another provider’s model, ideally under another jurisdiction, and/or an open model you can host yourself. The uncomfortable corollary is that your product must actually work in degraded mode. It’s not enough to have model B configured if your prompts, guardrails, and quality expectations were tuned only for model A. A product that only works at the state of the art is a product with no plan B.

3. Deliberate choice of capability tier. Here’s the episode’s most underrated lesson: Opus 4.8 and every other model stayed up. Only the absolute frontier fell — and that’s not a coincidence. Peak capability is exactly the regulatory-risk frontier, because that’s where the national-security-relevant capabilities live (offensive cyber first among them). For the overwhelming majority of real use cases, the prior generation delivers plenty of value at a fraction of the political risk. The design question isn’t “what’s the best model?” It’s “what’s the least capable model that still solves my problem?” — because that’s the one least likely to become a target.

4. Jurisdictional isolation as an architectural variable. The directive used nationality, not location. A foreign national in San Francisco fell alongside one in São Paulo. For anyone operating outside the U.S. — nearly the entire ecosystem here — that means treating provider jurisdiction as an explicit architectural variable, not an administrative footnote. Map it: how many distinct jurisdictions do my critical models span? Do I have at least one option that doesn’t depend on a Washington decision? Could I, in the limit, run something sovereign — an open model on my own infrastructure — for the flows that absolutely cannot stop? It doesn’t have to be plan A. It has to exist.

5. Contract and communication as part of the system. Technical continuity is moot if your customer finds out before you do. Two things that aren’t code but are product design: first, know what your provider contract says about interruption by government order (probably force majeure — meaning you’re on your own). Second, have a communication runbook ready: if the model drops at 5:21pm on a Friday, what message goes to customers, who triggers the fallback, how do you signal “degraded but standing.” Anthropic took hours to respond and promised details within 24. Your customers won’t wait 24 hours for yours.

The honest trade-off

None of this is free, and pretending otherwise would be dishonest. Abstraction, heterogeneous fallback, and portable prompts cost development speed and cap how deeply you can exploit any single model’s most specific features. There’s a real tension between extracting the maximum from today’s best model and not being hostage to any model.

Calibrate it by flow criticality. An experimental, optional feature can couple deeply to the model of the moment — if it falls, you disable the feature and move on. A flow that sustains the operation, where downtime means stalled customers and contracts at risk, earns the discipline of all five layers, even at a cost. Maturity isn’t armoring everything equally. It’s knowing, flow by flow, how much revocation risk you’re willing to carry.

The bottom line

Friday didn’t prove a government can shut off a model — that was theoretically obvious. It proved one does, with a minimal trigger and no warning, and that the shutdown is indiscriminate by construction. That reclassifies the frontier model: from a software dependency into a dual-use one, subject to a logic of containment — licenses, export controls, nationality restrictions — that the AI industry spent years ignoring while optimizing for maximum distribution.

For builders, the answer isn’t to avoid the good models. It’s to stop treating them as foundation and start treating them as an interchangeable part. The defensible product isn’t the one that uses the best model. It’s the one that survives swapping it on a Friday night — and the customer barely notices.

Daniel Braz is CTO & Head of the Digital Products Studio at BRQ Product & Experience Studios and CTO of Bud. A practitioner-researcher in agentic software engineering, he writes on harness engineering, spec-driven development, and how to build durable products on top of fast-moving — and, as of this week, revocable — frontier models.

Building on a model that can vanish on a Friday night was originally published in Level Up Coding on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: Three places to put the orchestrator
items.description:
Kimi’s swarm, Anthropic’s Dynamic Workflows, and the harness you build yourself — the same problem, three different control boundaries.

In my last piece (https://medium.com/@dsbraz/kimi-agent-swarm-when-one-model-becomes-a-team-fa4aa8710dba), I argued that the choice between Kimi K2.6’s Agent Swarm and a framework like LangGraph comes down to a single question: where do you want the control boundary. Model-native orchestration on one end — zero infrastructure, zero ability to audit the coordination logic. External orchestration on the other — full control, full engineering cost. Choose based on where you need control, not based on which approach is newer.

That framing had two points on the map.

Anthropic shipped Opus 4.8 last week with a feature called Dynamic Workflows, and it adds a third — one that does not fit cleanly on either end of the line I drew. So the map needs redrawing. The question is no longer “native or external.” It is which of three layers the orchestrator lives in.

1. The binary that is getting old

The Agent Swarm article set up a clean opposition, so let me recap it in one paragraph before breaking it.

On one end, model-native orchestration: Kimi decides how to decompose the task, which sub-agent profile fits each piece, how to manage shared state, and how to synthesize the result — all inside its own reasoning loop. One API call, one output, no infrastructure. The cost is that you cannot inspect or customize the orchestration logic. On the other end, orchestration you build: LangGraph, CrewAI, custom Python. You define the graph, the state transitions, the retry policy, the arbitration rules. Every agent turn is logged and inspectable. The cost is the engineering tax of building and maintaining that layer.

(The full treatment of the swarm side — the 300 sub-agents, the failure modes, the cost math — is in the previous article. I will not repeat it here.)

Two ends, one line. The trouble is that Dynamic Workflows lands in the middle, and the middle was supposed to be empty.

2. The third position: runtime-managed orchestration

Dynamic Workflows is orchestration that lives in the product runtime — outside the model, and outside your code.

That is the part worth sitting with. With the swarm, the orchestrator is the model itself, reasoning inside one inference run. With a framework, the orchestrator is software you wrote. Dynamic Workflows is neither. It is the Claude Code runtime planning the work, fanning it out across hundreds of parallel sub-agents, verifying the outputs, and reporting back. You do not write the planner. You also do not watch the model improvise it turn by turn. The orchestration is a managed capability of the tool you are calling.

The canonical example Anthropic put forward is a codebase-scale migration — hundreds of thousands of lines, kickoff to merge, with the existing test suite as the bar the work has to clear before it is considered done.

This is a genuinely different control boundary. In the swarm, the boundary sits at the edge of the model: everything inside the reasoning loop is opaque to you. In a framework, the boundary sits at the edge of your code: everything is yours. In Dynamic Workflows, the boundary sits at the edge of the product: you set the goal and the success criteria, the runtime owns the coordination, and what happens between those two is managed rather than authored.

3. Where each one puts the verifier

The clearest way to feel the difference between the three is to ask each one the same question: how does the system know it got the work right?

In the swarm, verification is a role inside the decomposition. You see this in Moonshot’s own examples — a Fact Checker sub-agent sits alongside the AI Researcher and the Physics Researcher. The orchestrator spawns a verifier the same way it spawns any other specialist. Verification is internal to the reasoning loop, and it is as auditable as the rest of that loop, which is to say: not very.

In Dynamic Workflows, verification is external to the reasoning. The test suite is the bar. The runtime can plan and parallelize however it likes, but the work is not done until the tests pass. This is a meaningfully stronger guarantee for a specific class of task, because the criterion is something you already own and trust — and it predates the agent. The agent does not get to define what “correct” means.

In the framework you build, verification is whatever you encode. Retry logic, arbitration between disagreeing agents, custom assertions tied to business invariants. Total control, total responsibility.

Three philosophies of correctness: a verifier that is one more agent, a verifier that is an external gate you already trusted, and a verifier that is exactly as good as the code you wrote for it.

4. Where each one came from, and why that shows

Architectures carry the fingerprints of the problems they were born solving.

The swarm grew out of broad search and research — BrowseComp, DeepSearchQA, matching one CV against a hundred job descriptions, generating thirty landing pages from maps data. That lineage explains its design choices. It is why the swarm is natively multimodal: real research tasks are not text-only, and a sub-agent reading a competitor’s landing page needs to see the layout. The swarm optimizes for heterogeneous parallel work across many independent workstreams.

Dynamic Workflows grew out of long-horizon software engineering. That lineage explains its anchor to the test suite as the unit of truth, and its headline example being a migration rather than a research report. It optimizes for sustained, verifiable progress on a single large codebase where correctness is sharply defined.

This origin story is not trivia. It predicts where each tool will disappoint you outside its home turf. Push the swarm into a task where one agent’s mutation silently invalidates another’s assumptions and you meet the state-drift problem head-on. Push Dynamic Workflows into a domain where “correct” cannot be expressed as a passing test and the verifier you were relying on has nothing to grip.

5. The divergence that actually matters: open versus closed

The three positions differ on infrastructure and on verification. But the divergence I find most consequential is about who is allowed to plug in.

Kimi’s answer is Claw Groups — a research preview that opens the swarm to heterogeneous agents. Other models, local deployments, mobile agents, each with its own toolkit and persistent memory, collaborating in a shared operational space with K2.6 as the adaptive coordinator. The bet is explicit: the harness becomes an open, multi-model protocol. The coordinator is Kimi, but the agents under it need not be.

Anthropic’s answer, so far, is the opposite. Dynamic Workflows lives inside the closed runtime of Claude Code. The orchestration is excellent and the sub-agents are Anthropic’s. There is no published notion of plugging a non-Anthropic agent into the workflow as a first-class collaborator.

So both houses did the same thing — pulled orchestration into a managed layer so you stop hand-rolling it — and then chose opposite boundaries for where that layer ends and who is allowed past it. One is betting the runtime becomes a shared protocol. The other is betting it becomes a proprietary product surface.

This is the same tension I wrote about in The Anatomy of an Agentic Platform: whether agent builders converge on a shared spec consumed by a common runtime, or whether each runtime stays a walled garden. Claw Groups and Dynamic Workflows are now two live, shipped answers to that question, pointing in different directions.

6. A note on asymmetric honesty

The Agent Swarm article had a section called “Where it breaks” — state drift, coordinator saturation, the cost math of a 300-agent run. I could write that section because Moonshot documented the architecture and third parties stress-tested it.

I cannot write the equivalent section for Dynamic Workflows yet, and I am not going to pretend otherwise. What exists today is Anthropic’s own material and early coverage. There is no independent evaluation of how Dynamic Workflows behaves under stress — no public account of its failure modes, no neutral benchmark putting its orchestration head to head with the swarm’s on the same task. The swarm numbers I cited (300 sub-agents, 4,000 coordinated steps, the speedups) are reported by Moonshot, but at least the swarm has been in the wild long enough for others to poke at it.

I use Claude in production. That is exactly why I am flagging this rather than skating past it. The evidence on the two sides is asymmetric right now, and giving the house whose model I run a free pass would be the easiest way to lose your trust. Treat section 2 through 5 as a structural comparison of design intent, not as a verdict on reliability. The reliability verdict needs data that does not exist yet.

7. Three places to put the orchestrator

So the map has three points now, not two. The decision is still about control, the way it was in the last article — there is just one more column in the middle.

Model-native (Kimi Agent Swarm). Reach for it when the task is parallelizable across heterogeneous workstreams, when you want it done with zero infrastructure, and when you do not need to audit how the coordination happened. You are trading transparency for convenience, knowingly.

Runtime-managed (Anthropic Dynamic Workflows). Reach for it when you want serious parallel orchestration without building it, and when your notion of “correct” can be handed to the runtime as a gate it must clear — a test suite, most naturally. You are trading authorship of the orchestration for a strong, externally-defined success criterion.

You build it (LangGraph and friends). Reach for it when agent decisions have to trace back to specific business rules, when auditors or security reviewers need every turn logged, when retries and arbitration must be deterministic. You are trading convenience for control, knowingly.

In our own platform, the pattern holds with the third column added in. Managed orchestration — whether model-native or runtime-level — works well for bounded, parallelizable workflows where the success criterion is clean. We build our own orchestration the moment agent decisions have to be traceable to a specific business rule, because no managed layer will explain itself to an auditor on your behalf.

The map gained a point. The advice did not change: choose based on where you need the control boundary to sit. There are just three places to put it now.

Daniel Braz is CTO/CPO at BRQ Digital Products Studio, shipping production agents and writing on agentic software engineering.

Three places to put the orchestrator was originally published in Level Up Coding on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: Kimi Agent Swarm: when one model becomes a team
items.description:
Native orchestration for parallel agent execution — and where it fits in your stack

1. The single-agent bottleneck

Most agent frameworks today run one model instance through a loop: plan, act, observe, repeat. This works for tasks with clear sequential dependencies — write code, run tests, fix errors. But it breaks when the task has parallelizable subproblems that require different specializations.

Consider a practical example: match one candidate CV against 100 job descriptions, generating a tailored resume for each role. A single agent must process descriptions one by one. At 100 iterations, latency becomes unacceptable. At 1,000, it becomes unusable.

The standard workaround is external orchestration — LangGraph, CrewAI, or custom Python workflows. You define the graph, state transitions, retry logic, and arbitration rules. This gives full control, but adds engineering overhead: infrastructure to build, debug, and maintain.

Kimi K2.6 takes a different path. It embeds swarm orchestration into the model itself.

2. What Agent Swarm changes

Kimi K2.6 can deploy up to 300 sub-agents in parallel, executing across 4,000 coordinated steps in a single autonomous run. This is a 3× increase from K2.5’s 100 agents and 1,500 steps.

The key difference is not the ceiling. It is that the decomposition, assignment, and aggregation happen inside the model’s reasoning loop — not in external code you write.

You make one API call with the goal. Kimi decides:
- How to break the task into parallelizable subtasks
- Which agent profile fits each subtask
- How to manage dependencies and shared state
- How to synthesize results into a unified output

For tasks with independent parallel workstreams, this achieves up to 4.5× speedup compared to sequential execution.

3. Three use cases that show the pattern

Moonshot has demonstrated concrete workloads that illustrate what this architecture enables:

Resume matching at scale. One uploaded CV was matched against 100 relevant roles in California. The swarm delivered 100 fully customized resumes in one run — each tailored to the specific job description.

Landing pages from maps data. Kimi identified 30 retail stores in Los Angeles without websites, then generated high-converting landing pages for each, including booking functionality synchronized to a database.

Research paper generation. An uploaded astrophysics paper was converted into a reusable skill. The swarm then produced a 40-page, 7,000-word research paper with a structured dataset of 20,000+ entries and 14 charts.

These are not demos. They are evidence that the swarm handles genuinely heterogeneous work: broad search, deep research, large-scale analysis, long-form writing, and multi-format content generation in a single coordinated execution.

4. Architecture: why native multimodal matters

Kimi K2.6 is built on a 1-trillion parameter Mixture-of-Experts architecture, activating 32 billion parameters per token. This keeps inference costs manageable while maintaining frontier-level quality.

The native multimodal design is critical. Trained on ~15 trillion mixed visual and text tokens, K2.6 processes text and images as a single modality from the start. The 400M-parameter MoonViT vision encoder enables agentic tasks that require visual understanding: analyzing UI mockups, processing screenshots, replicating website journeys from images.

This matters for the swarm because real-world tasks are rarely text-only. A sub-agent analyzing a competitor’s landing page needs to see the layout. A sub-agent debugging a UI needs to compare the mockup with the implementation. Native multimodal support means these agents do not need external vision pipelines — they see and reason in one pass.

5. Document-to-skill: reusable patterns at scale

K2.6 can convert any high-quality document into a reusable skill. PDFs, spreadsheets, slides, Word documents — the model captures their structural and stylistic DNA.

This is more powerful than simple template matching. Instead of prompting “write a report like this,” you upload the report. Kimi extracts the pattern: section structure, data visualization style, argument flow. Future tasks inherit that quality without starting from scratch.

When combined with Agent Swarm, your best work becomes infrastructure. A well-designed project template propagates across 300 parallel subtasks, ensuring consistency at scale.

6. Claw Groups: heterogeneous open swarms

The most significant architectural addition is Claw Groups. This extends the swarm beyond Kimi-native agents to heterogeneous agents — other models, local deployments, mobile agents — all collaborating in a shared operational space.

The design principle is explicit: multiple agents and humans operate as genuine collaborators. You onboard agents from any device, each with their own toolkits and persistent memory contexts. K2.6 serves as the adaptive coordinator, matching tasks to agents based on their specific skill profiles.

Moonshot uses this internally for content production: Demo Makers, Benchmark Makers, Social Media Agents, and Video Makers working in parallel, with K2.6 coordinating the pipeline.

This represents a shift from “AI does tasks for you” to “AI coordinates a team of heterogeneous agents on your behalf.”

7. Benchmarks that matter

On agentic evaluation, K2.6 delivers competitive results:

- Humanity’s Last Exam (Full) with tools: 54.0% — leading GPT-5.4 (52.1%), Claude Opus 4.6 (53.0%), and Gemini 3.1 Pro (51.4%)
- BrowseComp (Agent Swarm mode): 86.3% vs. 78.4% for K2.5
- SWE-Bench Verified: 80.2%
- SWE-Bench Pro: 58.6

HLE-Full with tools is particularly significant because it tests autonomous resource utilization — exactly what Agent Swarm is designed for.

8. Where it breaks

No honest assessment skips the failure modes. Three are worth understanding:

State drift. Over long runs, sub-agents can develop inconsistent views of shared state. Agent A modifies a data structure; Agent B operates on a stale snapshot. The shared operational space is designed to prevent this, but the specific consistency guarantees are not publicly documented. For coding tasks where multiple agents modify the same codebase concurrently, the risk of breaking invariants established by another agent is real.

Coordinator saturation. When the coordinator is busy re-planning a stalled subtask, it may deprioritize status updates from other agents. The coordinator is K2.6 itself, and its capacity is finite. Under high load, the entire swarm slows.

Cost scaling. A 300-agent swarm with 8,000 output tokens each consumes 2.4 million output tokens per run. At K2.6 API pricing, that is approximately $7.20 per execution. For tasks requiring multiple iterations, costs can exceed $50–100. Automatic caching of shared system prompts reduces input costs by 75–83%, but output token costs do not benefit from caching.

9. The control tradeoff

Agent Swarm represents a specific architectural choice.

K2.6 Agent Swarm is model-native orchestration. One API call, one synthesized output. Zero infrastructure overhead. Zero ability to audit or customize the orchestration logic.

External orchestration (LangGraph, custom frameworks) gives full control over graph structure, state transitions, retry logic, and arbitration. Every agent turn is logged and inspectable. The cost is engineering effort to build and maintain the orchestration layer.

The choice is not about which is universally better. It is about where you want the control boundary.

Teams that must explain agent decisions — to auditors, security reviewers, or colleagues debugging failures — will find external orchestration’s transparency worth the setup cost. Teams that need to move fast on parallelizable tasks with minimal infrastructure will find Agent Swarm’s convenience hard to beat.

In our own agent platform, we see a similar pattern: native orchestration works well for bounded, parallelizable workflows. External orchestration is necessary when agent decisions must be traceable to specific business rules.

10. Bottom line

Kimi K2.6’s Agent Swarm is not a replacement for orchestration frameworks. It is a zero-infrastructure alternative for tasks that benefit from parallel specialization.

If your workflow involves independent subtasks that require different skills — research, writing, analysis, generation — and you do not need fine-grained control over the coordination logic, Agent Swarm removes the engineering tax of building that orchestration yourself.

If you need auditability, custom retry policies, or integration with non-Kimi agents in a deterministic graph, external orchestration remains the right tool.

Choose based on where you need control, not based on which approach is newer.

Daniel Braz is CTO/CPO at BRQ Digital Products Studio, shipping production agents and writing on agentic software engineering.
